exam.fullName: National Eligibility cum Entrance Test - Postgraduate (NEET-PG)
exam.body: National Board of Examinations in Medical Sciences (NBEMS)
exam.purpose: Single qualifying-cum-ranking entrance for MBBS graduates seeking MD / MS / PG Diploma / DNB seats across India.
exam.sectional: 5 time-bound sections of 40 questions each, 42 minutes per section. Once a section's 42 minutes end it auto-closes; you cannot return to a previous section or jump ahead.
exam.imageQuestions: Roughly 18-25% of the paper is image/clinical-photo based (ECG, X-ray/CT/MRI, peripheral smear, histopathology, culture plates, fundus, derm photos, USG/CTG).
changes: 2024: time-bound sectional format (5 x 42 min); no backward navigation across sections.
Strong shift to clinical-vignette / next-best-step questions (~45-50%).
Applied basic-science: pre/para-clinical tested in clinical context.

weightageNote: Approximate questions per subject, proportional to the NBEMS blueprint and normalized to the current 200-question paper. NBEMS does not publish a fixed per-subject blueprint; clinical umbrellas (Medicine includes Derm/Psychiatry; Surgery includes Ortho/Anaesthesia/Radiology) are split out from coaching estimates.
strategy: The clinical big three (Surgery, Medicine, OBG) carry ~90+ marks together. Master these first, they are roughly a third of the paper.
Then lock the high-volume para-clinicals: Pathology (25), PSM (25), Pharmacology (20), Microbiology (20). Scoring and predictable.

Do not skip the short subjects (ENT, Ophthalmology, Forensic, Derm, Psychiatry, Ortho, Anaesthesia, Radiology, Pediatrics). Individually small but collectively 50+ marks, easy and high-ROI in the final two weeks.
Treat pre-clinicals (Anatomy/Physiology/Biochemistry, 50 marks) as applied subjects, learned through clinical correlation, not rote.
Solve every previous-year question you can find. Roughly a third of the paper is direct or modified repeats: the most reliable marks.
Adapt to the time-bound sections: budget ~1 minute per question. You cannot return to a section after 42 minutes, so flag-and-move within the section and revisit before the timer ends.
With -1 negative marking, attempt everything you know or can narrow to two options (positive expected value). Leave only true blanks.
Expect two close options. Build decision-making (diagnostic criteria, drug of choice, next best step), not isolated facts.
Practice image-based questions deliberately. Build a visual atlas of ECGs, X-rays, smears, histopath, fundus and derm photos.
Memorize the high-frequency criteria and scores (Jones, Duke, Rotterdam, Alvarado, CURB-65, GCS, Parkland, Child-Pugh, RIFLE) and the drug-of-choice tables. They recur every year.
Three-pass revision: concept clarity (5-6 months out), rapid tables/notes (2-3 months out), PYQs + full-length time-bound mocks (final month).
repeatedThemes: Drug of choice lists across Pharmacology
Culture media to organism matching (LJ-TB, Thayer-Martin, TCBS)
Tumor markers and their associated cancers
Hepatitis B serology / window period interpretation
ECG interpretation: MI localization and arrhythmias
Postpartum hemorrhage management ladder and the 4 Ts
Magnesium sulfate regimens (Pritchard / Zuspan) for eclampsia
Parkland formula for burns fluid resuscitation
Nerve injuries with specific fractures (radial-humerus shaft)
Rotterdam, Jones and Duke criteria
National immunization schedule and new vaccines (PCV, RVV, MR)
Sensitivity / specificity / PPV / NPV calculations
Anti-tubercular regimens and drug-induced hepatotoxicity
Hematological malignancies (Auer rods, Philadelphia chromosome, Reed-Sternberg)
Amyloidosis: Congo red apple-green birefringence
Rinne and Weber tuning-fork interpretation
Pemphigus vs pemphigoid (Nikolsky, IF pattern)
Organophosphorus poisoning and atropine/pralidoxime
IPC sections (304A, 304B, 376)
Embryological anomalies (TE fistula, Meckel's, branchial arch derivatives)
Oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve shifts (Bohr effect)
DKA vs HHS differentiation
Storage diseases enzyme deficiencies (Gaucher, Tay-Sachs, Pompe)
